adversarial_results.detail: Validator returned clear error: 'YAML root must be a mapping'. Exit code 1.
Validator returned clear error: "'utf-8' codec can't decode byte 0x91 in position 3". Exit code 1.
Validated successfully with no issues. Parser handles deeply nested structures fine.
YAML parse error returned: 'mapping values are not allowed here'. The extremely long key triggers a YAML parser limitation. Exit code 1.
YAML parse error: 'unacceptable character #x0000: special characters are not allowed'. Null bytes are rejected at the YAML parser level. Exit code 1.

adversarial_results.description: Shell injection via input value with quote escaping
adversarial_results.detail: Input value 'hello"; touch /tmp/.../pwned; echo "done' successfully broke out of double-quoted context in the run: script and executed arbitrary commands. The /tmp/.../pwned file was created, confirming command execution. Root cause: shlex.quote() wraps values in single quotes, which are ineffective inside bash double-quoted strings. When flow authors write run: 'echo "$var"' (natural pattern), injected double-quotes close the DQ context and semicolons become command separators.
YAML with 8 levels of 10x anchor expansion (10^8 potential items) validated successfully and quickly. Python's YAML library handles anchor resolution efficiently. No observable memory or CPU spike.

adversarial_results.description: Config interpolation injection via $(command) and backticks
adversarial_results.detail: Both $(whoami) and `whoami` execute when interpolated into double-quoted shell contexts in run: scripts. Results showed the actual username 'zack' wrapped in literal single quotes from shlex.quote(). The shlex.quote() protection is ineffective because: (1) single quotes are literal inside bash DQ strings, (2) both $() and `` are command-substitution operators inside DQ strings. Direct env var access (STEPWISE_INPUT_<name>) is safe — the vulnerability is specifically in the $variable template interpolation path.
Engine enforces a 5MB artifact size limit. Clear error: 'Step artifact too large: 10.0MB (limit: 5MB). Reduce output size or split into smaller steps.' Job failed gracefully.
Validated 1000-step chain in 0.245 seconds. No performance issues. Output: '✓ /tmp/chain1000.flow.yaml (1000 steps)'.

adversarial_results.description: Rapid job creation (50 concurrent API requests)
adversarial_results.detail: 50 concurrent POST requests to /api/jobs completed in 23ms total. Server remained responsive after the burst. FastAPI + SQLite WAL mode handled concurrent writes without locking issues.

Validator detected the cycle: 'Cycle detected: self-step → (input: data) self-step. Consider adding optional: true to one input binding to break the cycle.' Both validate and run rejected with clear errors.
Validated and ran successfully. Step output was captured as {stdout: 'hello world'} rather than matched against declared outputs. Job completed normally.

adversarial_results.description: Special characters in job inputs (newlines, tabs, quotes, backslashes)
adversarial_results.detail: All special characters preserved correctly through the pipeline. Input with newlines, tabs, double quotes, and backslashes round-tripped via STEPWISE_INPUT_<name> env var and appeared in output artifact with correct escaping.

critical_findings.description: Shell injection via double-quote escape in input values
critical_findings.detail: When a flow's run: script embeds $variable inside double-quoted bash expressions (e.g., echo "$var"), an attacker-controlled input value containing double quotes can break out of the quoting context and execute arbitrary shell commands. shlex.quote() is applied but produces single-quoted output, which is ineffective inside bash DQ strings. Proof: input value created the file /tmp/pwned via command injection.

critical_findings.description: Command substitution injection via backticks and $() in input values
critical_findings.detail: Both `command` and $(command) syntax in input values are executed as command substitution when interpolated into double-quoted shell contexts via $variable in run: scripts. shlex.quote()'s single-quote wrapping is ineffective inside bash DQ strings. Proof: input '$(whoami)' and '`whoami`' both returned the actual username 'zack'. Mitigation: pass values exclusively via STEPWISE_INPUT_<name> env vars (which are safe) or apply DQ-aware escaping (escape ", $, `, \ in values destined for shell command keys).
